question: My spouse earns income from freelance consulting and also receives a pension. How do I assess whether her total income falls under the threshold for me to remain entitled to the household allowance?
answer: The household allowance is payable only where the gross annual income of the member of staff's spouse or recognised partner does not exceed EUR 71,764. For this purpose, "gross annual income" is defined as income originating in the calendar year preceding that in respect of which eligibility is assessed, from employment or self-employment, from income replacement (e.g. maternity allowance, parental allowance, social security benefits and unemployment allowance, but not payments representing reimbursement of expenses), or from a pension, before tax and before the deduction of social security contributions; investment income and income from capital gains are not considered. Both the freelance consulting income and the pension would therefore count toward the threshold. (ECB Conditions of Employment, page 9; ECB Staff Rules, page 63)

question: How many days of annual leave am I entitled to each year, and what are the rules for carrying unused days over to the following year?
answer: Members of staff are entitled to thirty and a half working days of paid leave per annum, in addition to the public holidays observed by the ECB. At least 20 days must be taken before the end of the calendar year, and members of staff may carry over a maximum of 10.5 days of the annual leave entitlement to the following year. Where the minimum 20 days have not been taken by year-end for business or personal reasons, the Director General/Director may approve them being taken until the end of March of the subsequent year, and the Director General Human Resources or their Deputy may approve a further unlimited carry-over in exceptional cases on local management's justification. (ECB Conditions of Employment, page 13; ECB Staff Rules, page 93)

question: I'm joining the ECB on an indefinite contract next month. How long will my probationary period be and under what circumstances can it be extended?
answer: Appointments shall be subject to a probationary period of six months unless the Chief Services Officer, on behalf of the Executive Board, decides to waive it. Where the probationer is prevented by illness, accident, maternity or, in exceptional circumstances, special leave from performing their duties for a period of more than one month, the probationary period may be extended accordingly; in addition, in exceptional circumstances the Chief Services Officer may extend the probationary period up to a maximum of twelve months, with or without assigning the probationer to another function. The probationary period shall in no circumstances exceed twelve months, and a member of staff shall not be subject to more than one probationary period for the same or an equivalent position. (ECB Conditions of Employment, page 6; ECB Staff Rules, pages 47–48)

question: I'm expecting my first child in five months. How much maternity leave will I receive, and when am I able to start it?
answer: Members of staff are entitled to 20 weeks of paid maternity leave, with an additional four weeks in the event of a multiple birth. On application by the member of staff, maternity leave shall start between three and six weeks before the expected date of confinement, and shall end when the period laid down in the Conditions of Employment has elapsed, calculated from the date on which the maternity leave starts or the actual date of confinement, whichever occurs first. The medical certificate stating the expected date of confinement must be sent to the Directorate General Human Resources at least three months before that date. (ECB Conditions of Employment, page 13; ECB Staff Rules, pages 94–95)

question: I'm in salary band C and my line manager asked me to work several hours last Saturday because of a publication deadline. If I take payment instead of compensatory leave, how is the amount calculated?
answer: For members of staff with a basic salary up to step 59 of salary band E, overtime worked on Saturdays, Sundays and public holidays is compensated at two hours for each hour of overtime worked. Where compensatory leave for overtime cannot be granted, the member of staff may instead be granted overtime payment in lieu of leave, and the standard hourly rate for such payment is 0.6% of the basic monthly salary for the month in which the overtime was worked. (ECB Conditions of Employment, page 13; ECB Staff Rules, pages 89–90)
